nativeRange.habitat: Outer reef slopes and drop-offs in current-swept areas, typically at depths of roughly 12–81 m

diet.notes: Genicanthus is the only zooplanktivorous genus among the angelfishes. In the wild has been documented feeding on plankton in the water column above current-swept reef slopes. In aquariums, generally advised to offer a varied diet of vitamin-enriched mysis and brine shrimp, finely-chopped meaty foods, Spirulina, and marine algae preparations, fed several times per day.

summary: The Watanabei Angelfish is a sexually dimorphic, peaceful planktivore from the tropical Pacific, generally considered reef-safe and commonly advised for larger reef aquariums with strong current and open swimming space.
careNotes: Genicanthus watanabei belongs to the only planktivorous genus within the angelfish family Pomacanthidae, so it is generally considered safer around corals and sessile invertebrates than most other marine angelfishes. The species is strongly sexually dimorphic: males display a pale blue-grey body with about eight horizontal black stripes on the lower two-thirds of the body, while females are overall light blue with a wide black bar across the head and a black spot on the snout. In the wild it tends to form harems of 2–5 individuals (one dominant male with several females) on outer reef slopes and drop-offs in strong current. Only one male should be kept per aquarium — males have been known to fight to the death. A male–female pair or single male with several females is the recommended configuration. The species generally requires ample swimming space and open water above the rockwork; a tank with a long footprint and good flow is commonly recommended. Has been reported to be susceptible to swim-bladder issues during shipping, so a dim acclimation environment is generally advised. Temperature spread noted between sources: LiveAquaria cites 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C) while FishBase preferred range is 24.2–28.7°C; the averaged range of 23.2–27.2°C is used here.
breedingNotes: Genicanthus watanabei is a protogynous hermaphrodite — individuals begin life as females and the dominant female may transition into a male if no male is present. Wild groups follow a lek-style mating system with dominant males controlling territories. Captive breeding has been reported but remains uncommon in the hobby; the species spawns pelagic eggs that are difficult to rear.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (15.0 cm TL), preferred temperature (24.2–28.7°C, mean 26.5°C), depth (21–81 m), native range, taxonomy, and trophic level (3.4 ± 0.45, plankton feeder). Confirmed harems of 2–5 individuals. Temperature averaged with LiveAquaria's 22.2–25.6°C to give 23.2–27.2°C used in the file.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Pomacanthidae, Acanthuriformes), max length 15 cm, distribution Taiwan east to Tuamotus including Great Barrier Reef, depth 12–81 m, protogynous hermaphroditism, and lek-style mating system.

fish.breedingNotes: Protogynous hermaphrodite with lek mating; captive breeding reported but uncommon. Pelagic eggs and larvae are difficult to rear.
